goal: Restructure README: drop never-used commands (canonical-hash, reconcile alias, thread) from main table, split commands into Daily vs Advanced, add TOC + FAQ + Configuration sections, update with real dogfood data point (3 phase1 pairs, 0 false positives)

summary.title: README restructure: drop never-used commands, add TOC + FAQ + Configuration + Roadmap
summary.what: Top-down rewrite of README.md to match what real readers want. New layout: tagline + sample log output → TOC → Why Mainline (problem statement) → Install → five-minute quick start → workflow ASCII diagram → two-store architecture (kept user's text verbatim) → concepts (intent / turn / fingerprint / phase 1 vs phase 2 / pin) → Daily commands (12) → Advanced commands (8) → Configuration → FAQ (5 questions) → Storage layout → Development → Project structure → Roadmap. The split between Daily and Advanced moves canonical-hash, reconcile (deprecated), thread, list-proposals, publish, pr-description, manual pin, and merge out of the main table — none are part of the typical human or agent loop. Adds a Configuration section that names the four settings worth knowing (phase1_threshold, freshness_seconds, stale_threshold_seconds, auto_check_after_sync) instead of dumping the whole TOML schema.
summary.why: Dogfood feedback: the previous README's command table listed every command as if all were equal, including pure debug surfaces like `mainline canonical-hash` that no human or agent will ever invoke. A new reader landing on the table cannot tell which commands matter. The restructure lets a reader scan top-to-bottom and learn the system in roughly 5 minutes (Why → Quick start → How it fits) without dipping into the advanced surface, then come back to the advanced section only when they hit a non-default workflow. Adding the FAQ codifies questions that came up repeatedly in dogfood ("why no merge?", "what does check:? mean?", "do I need to sync manually?") so newcomers do not need to reverse-engineer the answers from the spec patches.
summary.user_goal: Make the README scannable for someone who has never used Mainline — and honest about which commands are daily vs marginal.

decisions.point: Where canonical-hash, reconcile, thread, etc. live
decisions.chose: Move out of the main commands table into a separate 'Advanced commands' table
decisions.rationale: These commands are real (some are deprecated, some are debug, some are for non-PR pipelines) but listing them next to `mainline init` and `mainline status` made every command look equally important. Demoting them shortens the daily-use cognitive load while keeping a discoverable home for the rest.
rejected: Drop them from the README entirely — "undocumented but accepted" CLI surfaces are worse than "documented as advanced".
Hide canonical-hash since it really is internal — but it appears in --help output, so README must mention it for grep-from-help users.

decisions.point: Adding a TOC at the top
decisions.rationale: README is now 280+ lines. Without a TOC, a reader who lands midway has no map. Hand-maintained beats auto-generated for a docs-only file with stable headings.

decisions.point: Sample log output as the second visible block
decisions.chose: Show real-looking output with a [check:~] callout arrow
decisions.rationale: READMEs that lead with a screenshot or sample output get 'aha, that's what it does' across in 10 seconds. The arrow on the [check:~] row demonstrates the rc6 column without forcing the reader into the concepts section first.

decisions.chose: Five questions from real dogfood — why no merge, what does ? mean, when to run phase 2, do I need manual sync, what if pin lands on wrong commit, can two intents share one commit
decisions.rationale: Each is a question I or the reviewer actually asked during this dogfood week. Synthetic FAQs drift toward marketing copy; real ones earn their lines.

decisions.chose: rc1 → rc6 + v0.2 + v0.5 with explicit Implemented / Planned / Superseded markers
decisions.rationale: External readers want to know what's shipping vs aspirational. The rc-by-rc breakdown also doubles as a quick history of how the design evolved — useful for anyone who reads the spec patches afterwards.

risks: TOC anchors are fragile under heading renames — every later restructure must check anchors still resolve.

The Roadmap milestone table claims rc6 is 'Implemented' which is true on main today but the rc6 spec patch document hasn't been written; eventually we should backfill mainline-spec-v0.1-rc6-patch.md to match what shipped.
followups: Generate the rc6 spec patch document covering [check:X] cascade + phase1-warnings cache + ReadTeamConfig backfill — captures the design intent we shipped without spec-first discipline.
Add a real demo asciinema or animated GIF in the README showing the seal --submit → conflict warning → reviewer pin loop end-to-end.

Once we have telemetry on phase1 hit rate, surface 'real-world performance' callout in the Why section ('In dogfood: 3 phase1 pairs across 16 intents, 0 false positives, threshold 0.10 caught the 0.103 borderline case').

architectural_claims: Daily-use commands and advanced/debug commands are documented in separate tables.
Quick start and How-it-fits sections together teach the system in <5 min reading time.
